coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient As(III) removal through simultaneous oxidation and adsorption by N-CQDs modified MIL-53(Fe)
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Haoyun
Yuan, Xingzhong
Jiang, Longbo
Wang, Hou
Zeng, Guanjun
coredata.dc:description: It has been ever attracting increasingly growing demand to develop highly efficient method of aqueous arsenites [As(III)] removal due to the high toxicity of arsenic in drinking water. This work presents an oil vine-like structural MIL-53(Fe) modified by nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with excellent As(III) removal property, which is achieved through the photocatalytic oxidation of the As(III) and simultaneous adsorbed the generated arsenate [As(V)]. The abilities of As(III) oxidation and removal efficiency for N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) are over 6 times higher than that of pure MIL-53(Fe). The impressively capability of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) in photocatalytic oxidation and adsorption is attributed to the accelerated separation of photogenerated electron-hole by the in situ embedded N-CQDs. Moreover, the results of the experimental analysis and density functional theory (DFT) DFT calculations confirm that the edge structure of C/O site significantly improves the removal ability of As(III) by forming bidentate C-O-As bonds and occupying coordinatively unsaturated atoms. Therefore, based on the excellent performance and reusability of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) in As(III) removal, it offers a new potential strategy to develop high efficient MOF based materials for As(III) removal.

(a-b) SEM image of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe). (c-d) TEM image of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe). (e-f) HTEM image of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe).

(a) UV–vis DRS spectra and (b) Bandgap energies of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe). Mott-Schottky plots of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) and (d) VB XPS spectrum of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe). (e) Transient photocurrent responses of pure MIL-53(Fe) and N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) composites. (f) EIS Nyquist plots of pure MIL-53(Fe) and N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) composites.

Concentration of (c) total As and (d) As(III)as a function of pH in the presence of NQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) composite. Effect of coexisting ions (e) cation and (f) anion by NQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) composite at pH 6.9. Reaction conditions: [As(III)] = 10 mg L−1, [photocatalyst] = 10 mg L−1.

(a) PL spectra of N-CQDs/ MIL-53(Fe) samples. (b) Trapping experiments (Capture agent: 0.5 mM/L) for arsenic removal. ESR spectra for N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) composite (c) methanol dispersion for DMPO- O2 − and (d) aqueous dispersion for DMPO- OH.
Adsorption of As(III) on site-1 (a), site-2 (b), site-3 (c) and site-4 (d) of N-CQDs/MIL-53(Fe)’s surface, respectively, and the corresponding free energy (eV) of these conformations.
Graphical presentation of the As(III) photocatalytic oxidation and sorption mechanism on N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe).

head.ce:title: Highly efficient As(III) removal through simultaneous oxidation and adsorption by N-CQDs modified MIL-53(Fe)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The oil vine-like structural of MIL-53(Fe) decorated by N-CQDs exhibits brilliant abilities of As(III) removal efficiency which is achieved through photocatalytic oxidating the As(III) and simultaneous adsorbing the generated arsenate [As(V)]. In situ embed N-CQDs into MIL-53(Fe) was proved to lead an increased charge transportation and improved utilization of the electrons and holes and the edge structure of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) manifested the significantly As(III) removal ability.

It has been ever attracting increasingly growing demand to develop highly efficient method of aqueous arsenites [As(III)] removal due to the high toxicity of arsenic in drinking water. This work presents an oil vine-like structural MIL-53(Fe) modified by nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with excellent As(III) removal property, which is achieved through the photocatalytic oxidation of the As(III) and simultaneous adsorbed the generated arsenate [As(V)]. The abilities of As(III) oxidation and removal efficiency for N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) are over 6 times higher than that of pure MIL-53(Fe). The impressively capability of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) in photocatalytic oxidation and adsorption is attributed to the accelerated separation of photogenerated electron-hole by the in situ embedded N-CQDs. Moreover, the results of the experimental analysis and density functional theory (DFT) DFT calculations confirm that the edge structure of C/O site significantly improves the removal ability of As(III) by forming bidentate C-O-As bonds and occupying coordinatively unsaturated atoms. Therefore, based on the excellent performance and reusability of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) in As(III) removal, it offers a new potential strategy to develop high efficient MOF based materials for As(III) removal.

ce:section.ce:para: Arsenic (As) is one of the most hazardous and long-term contaminants which is widely presented in groundwater [1]. It is induced into aquatic environments through both natural processes and anthropogenic activities such as smelting, burning, and arsenic compounds consumption [2,3]. Due to its intensely carcinogenicity and toxicity, arsenic pollution is ever attracting increasing concerns. Over 230 regions distributed in more than 70 countries in the world are facing the severer arsenic pollutions leading to the arsenic removal an urgent and global problem demanding for solution [4]. Technologies including ion exchanges, adsorption, and precipitation have been developed to eliminate arsenic from water [4]. Among which adsorption is proven to be one of the most effective techniques to remove the arsenic from water environment. Multiple adsorbents including zeolite, red mud mesoporous alumina, acid-modified carbon black have been reported for As elimination [5]. However, most of the adsorbents have a low adsorption capacity for trivalent arsenic (As(III)) which is more toxic than pentavalent arsenic (As(V)) due to its low affinity for adsorbents and high mobility [6]. Therefore, oxidating the As(III) to As(V) and simultaneously adsorbing the generated As(V) can be a significantly promising combined technology to achieve high arsenic removal efficiency. For example, Wang and colleagues reported a metal–organic framework (MIL-100(Fe)) filter for efficient remediation of Arsenic (As) contamination in groundwater with low concentration [7]. Zhang et.al and Pang et.al presented a Fenton reaction to promote the oxidation of As(III) to As(V) over a few hours [8,9]. Thereby, developing a higher efficient oxidation process still requires great efforts. The photocatalytic oxidation process is recognized as one of the most effective and environmentally acceptable techniques [10]. In this case, finding bifunctional material to achieve both oxidation and adsorption processes for arsenic removal is going to be crucial.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are well recognized as crystalline porous materials which have emerged with great interest in using MOFs to address solar-driven photocatalytic reactions. MOFs have also been proposed as the most promising adsorption material due to their diverse structures, highly tailored porosities, and large specific surface areas [11,12]. Motivated by the prospects of the applications in adsorption and photocatalysis, a wide variety of MOFs are reported with large micropore volumes, stable structure, and abundant available reaction sites [13]. Amon which, MIL-53(Fe) with limited recombination of the photogenerated charge carriers and smaller Fe-O cluster size is expected to achieve superior photoactivities [14]. For example, a rod-like crystal of MIL-53(Fe) was reported to photodegradation the sulfamethazine assisted by Fe(III) [11]. Another work prepared the BiOBr/MIL-53(Fe) composites to photocatalytic degradation rhodamine B and carbamazepine demonstrating the photodegradation ability of MIL-53(Fe) [13]. However, their photocatalytic performance is still not better enough for widely application due to the rapid electron/hole recombination and low solar energy conversion efficiency. Therefore, it is of great importance to modify MOFs with the proper method to take the advantage of pore structures as well as expose plenty of active sites.

Owing to the ability to inject photoexcited electrons into the conduction bands of most metal oxide-based materials, quantum dots (QDs) are replacing conventional coupling mediate being introduced into photocatalysis to enhance the photocatalytic activity in recent years [15,16]. Among which, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have gained substantial progress due to their reservoir properties and excellent electron transfer [17]. Promoted by the quantum-confinement effects, nitrogen doped CQDs (N-CQDs) with unique physical and chemical properties have been reported [18]. Moreover, there are abundant functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs providing many favorable photocatalytic reaction sites [19]. Under the exciting of visible light, the excited-state of the N-CQDs can be deconvoluted into hole and electron contributions. Hence, the behavior of the photocatalytic performance can be significantly improved with the excited quantum largely exposed to the physical boundary on the surface of quantum dots to modify surface electronic states [20]. Thereby, embedding N-CQDs can further improve the photocatalytic performance through promoting intermolecular electron transfer or building surface separation centers for electrons and reducing the recombination rate of charge carriers. Thus, it is worthwhile to explore the information of internal embedding N-CQDs into MOFs to improve the photocatalytic performance.

To the best of our knowledge, there are paucity reports on the fabrication of in situ embedding N-CQDs into MOFs, and the light stimulated electron transfer between the N-CQDs and MOFs remains uninvestigated. Besides, injecting N-CQDs into MOFs and studying the sensitized hole transfer from photoexcited N-CQDs to the valence bands of MOFs will aid both the fundamental understanding and practical development of improved photocatalytic conversion systems. Moreover, the synthesis of N-CQDs decorated olive-like MIL-53(Fe) via one-step hydrothermal method and its usage as a bifunctional photocatalyst/adsorbent have not been reported. In this work, we showed an in situ embedding introduction of N-CQDs into MIL-53(Fe). The effects of coexisting ions and solution pH on the removal of As(III) were also discussed. The properties of As removal mechanism on N-CQDs decorated MIL-53(Fe) were obtained through detailed characterizations combining with calculation results. The excellent enhanced performance of the modulated MIL-53(Fe) opens the gate for advancing MOFs in arsenic removal.

All the chemical reagents were received with analytical purity or higher without needing further purification. The regents of Ammonium citrates, Ethylenediamine N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99%), 1,4-Benzenedicarboxylic acid (H2BDC, 99%), Ethylene glycol (EG, 99.5%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). p-benzoquinone (BQ), Sodium oxalate (SO), Isopropanol (IPA, 99%), and Iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O, 99%), (greater than250 U/mg) were obtained from Macklin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).

The N-CQDs were prepared through a hydrothermal process [19]. As a typical procedure in the reported literature, 10 mmol ammonium citrates and 670 μL ethylenediamine were added into 20 mL deionized water. After stirring for 30 min, the solution was transferred to 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. Then, it was heated for 5 h at 200 ℃. After cooling down to the room temperature naturally, the obtained sample was subjected to dialysis (MWCO 1000) for 24 h. Eventually, the N-CQDs solution was obtained. To obtain the concentration of as-prepared N-CQDs solution, 20 mL N-CQDs solution was freeze dried the N-CQD solution to get a dry N-CQD sample [21].

The MIL-53(Fe) was fabricated according to a facile solvothermal method as reported [18]. 230 mg FeCl3·6H2O, 370 mg H2BDC and 60 mL DMF were mixed and then stirred for an hour until the uniform and clear solution formed. Thereafter, the reacted mixture was transferred into the Teflon-lined stainless steel followed by heating to 150 °C and sustained for 6 h period. The suspension was centrifugated at 8000 r/min over 5 min to separate out the precipitate. After being washed with ethylene glycol over three times, the obtained precipitate was dried over a night at 60 °C under vacuum. The preparation process for N-CQDs/MIL-53(Fe) is shown in Scheme 1 . Similar to the synthesis process of MIL-53(Fe), 230 mg FeCl3·6H2O and 370 mg H2BDC were mixed in 60 mL DMF. After stirring for 30 min, a certain amount of N-CQDs solution (1 mL, 3 mL and 5 mL) was added into the as-prepared mixture solution. The followed process was just like the MIL-53(Fe) fabrication. The synthesized powder was collected and marked as N-CQDs1/MIL-53(Fe), N-CQDs2/MIL-53(Fe) and N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe).

The arsenic removal efficiency of N-CQDs/MIL-53(Fe) for As(III) removal was carried out in a 100 mL aqueous solution containing 20 mg/L As(III). Detailed procedure and analytical methods were presented in supporting information.

The geometry and single point energy of periodic MIL-53(Fe)/N-CQDs were calculated. The calculations were carried out using density functional theory (DFT) at the level of generalized-gradient approximation (GGA) with exchange–correlation functional of Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE), which was provided by the CASTEP program [22–24]. The maximum force in geometry optimization was 0.15 eV/Å, the energy cutoff was 400 eV. The MIL-53(Fe) (110) surface was selected as the loading platform of N-CQDs, and a vacuum of 15 Å was added. The Monkhorst-Pack k-point grid for Brillouin zone sampling was 2 × 1 × 1 to optimize the equilibrium lattice constants, to be to be a = 13.7676 Å, b = 20.57761 Å, c = 18.5842 Å, α = 90.0°, β = 90.0°, γ = 95.5178°. This surface model with p(2 × 1) periodicity in the × and y di Rection and 2 stoichiometric layers in the z di Rection separated by a vacuum layer in the depth of 15 Å to separate the slab from its periodic duplicates. The electron density difference (EDD) analysis was carried out for identification of the binding interaction between MIL-53(Fe) and NQCDs. The k-point was determined as fine (2 × 1 × 1) for obtaining results of partial density of states (PDOS). The free energy of As(III) adsorption on various sites of N-CQDs/MIL-53(Fe)’s surface was explored. The relevant images were obtained by using VESTA program [25].

The crystallographic structures of the pure MIL-53(Fe) and after embedding with the N-CQDs were firstly identified by X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns (shown in Fig. 1 a and Fig S2a). The obtained diffraction peaks at 2θ = 9.2, 12.4, 17.5, and 25.1° exhibited high consistency of the characteristic diffraction peaks with those of previously reported patterns suggesting the successful synthesis of MIL-53(Fe) [26]. Compare with the pure MIL-53(Fe), the characteristic diffraction peaks were also observed in the N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) composites. It was also noticed that the peak intensity in the 2θ values of (110) was weaker than that of MIL-53(Fe) which may cause by the sheltering of the crystals lattice in the mixture after the embedding with N-CQDs. This phenomenon has also been reported in other N-CQDs modified composites [27]. In specifically, a small shift at 2θ = 9.2 towards high degree was observed in N-CQDs modified MIL-53(Fe) which is attributed to the separating of Fe-O cluster and occupied by some other interstitial sites [13]. This result further confirmed the successful embedding of N-CQDs into the framework [27]. FTIR spectroscopy was conducted to confirm the surface groups in the MIL-53(Fe) and N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) and the results were displayed in Fig. 1b and Fig S2b. There were weaker and small bands that could be observed at 430, 445, 545, and 560 cm−1 over the wavenumber range of 450–4000 cm−1 for bare MIL-53(Fe) and N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) composites. These weaker and small bands can be ascribed to the stretching modes of FeO on tetrahedral and octahedral sites which assuring the presence of metal-oxo bond [28]. The peaks at 1403 and 1540 cm−1 corresponding to the asymmetric (CO) and symmetric (CO) vibrations demonstrating the construction of dicarboxylate linker [28]. The absorption bands at 2862 and 2935 cm−1 were attributed to the symmetric and asymmetric CH bonding vibration on the framework while the broad peak observed at 3409 cm−1 were allocated to the stretching vibrations of O-H over water molecules [29]. It was worth to notice that an emerging medium absorption peak at 485 cm−1 could be ascribed to the characteristic peak of stretching vibrations of N-CQDs confirming the existence of N-CQDs in the N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) composite [30]. The surface composition and chemical states of the MIL-53(Fe) and N-CQDs/MIL-53(Fe) were further compared through the XPS spectra. As displayed in Fig. 2 , the predominant elements on the surface of MIL-53(Fe) were C, O and Fe elements, while C, O, Fe and N elements were for N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe). The C 1 s spectra of MIL-53(Fe) could be split into two peaks at 284.6 and 288.4 eV corresponding to the C-C and O-CO respectively [31]. The C 1 s peaks for N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) were integrated by three peaks located at 284.8, 286.2 and 288.4 eV which could be ascribed to the C-C, CN, and OCO bonds [32]. Comparing the high-resolution O 1 s spectrum of MIL-53(Fe) and N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe), two peaks at 532.26 and 531.56 eV were originated from Fe-O bones and C-O components [33]. Two typical peaks at 711.7 and 725.4 eV of Fe 2p spectra shown in Fig. 2c were belong to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 respectively, which were well in accordance with the reported characteristics peaks [34]. In particular, the Fe 2p peaks of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) composites shifted to higher binding energy demonstrating the interaction between MIL-53(Fe) and N-CQDs in the N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) composites [35]. Moreover, there were two splitting peaks of N 1 s only presented in N-CQDs/MIL-53(Fe) located at 400.6 and 399.3 eV which could be assigned to CN further demonstrating the successful introduction of N-CQDs into MIL-53(Fe) [36]. Combing the XRD, XPS and FTIR results, it could be elucidated that the N-CQDs was not only physically adsorbed but also chemically interacted embedding into MIL-53(Fe). To further demonstrate the chemically embedding N-CQDs into MIL-53(Fe), DFT calculation was conducted to analyze the electron density difference (EDD). As displayed in Fig. S1, the model of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) composite was reasonably designed where the (110) surface of MIL-53(Fe) was selected as the loading platform of N-CQDs, and a vacuum of 15 Å was added. The EDD properties were obtained after geometry optimization the composite model. It was speculated from Fig.S1, the surface charge density of N-CQDs mainly exhibits the reducing characteristics while the MIL-53(Fe) mainly shows the characteristics of increased charge density. The surface charge density was obviously accumulated at the interface junction of N-CQDs and MIL-53(Fe) where the electrons were driven flow to MIL-53(Fe) from the N-CQDs. The preferable electrons accumulated on the MIL-53(Fe) transferring through the interface from N-CQDs deduced the strongly interacted electron density between the N-CQDs and MIL-53(Fe) [37]. This result further elevated that N-CQDs was chemically interacted embedding into MIL-53(Fe).

The morphologies and interfacial structures of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) were directly testified by SEM and TEM and the results were shown in Fig. 3 . It was clearly depicted that a unique oil vine-like structural was formed after embedding with the N-CQDs which was in part different from the previous reported MIL-53(Fe). The final structure and morphology of the N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) were greatly decided by the nucleation and growth process. The altered structure (Fig. 3) illustrated that introducing N-CQDs might alter the equilibrium of the organic ligand linkage with metal ions which was consistent with the XRD result. N-CQDs was observed to be distributed in the structure of MIL-53(Fe) from the TEM images. The obvious particles within 10 nm in Fig S3 confirmed the successfully synthesized pure N-CQDs. The formed intimate interface between MIL-53(Fe) and N-CQDs was further illustrated by HTEM image performed in Fig. 3e. It was clearly shown that the crystal lattice fringe distances for MIL-53(Fe) and N-CQDs were 0.14 and 0.17 nm respectively which further demonstrated the successful introduction of N-CQDs into MIL-53(Fe).

The optical absorption properties of the MIL-53(Fe) and N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) samples were investigated by conducting the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS). And the band structures were studied by Mott-Schottky plots. As presented in Fig. 4 a, the pure MIL-53(Fe) exhibited an absorption edge situated at about 600 nm. After in-situ embedding N-CQDs, obvious affection on absorption edge could be observed with the photo-absorption range extending to 700 nm. The obviously redshift illustrated the addition of N-CQDs could significantly affect the optical property of MIL-53(Fe). Transformed from the Kubelka-Munk function, the bandgap energies of the pure MIL-53(Fe) and N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) samples were calculated. As displayed in Fig. 4b, the band gap for pure MIL-53(Fe) was 2.67 eV. With the embedding of N-CQDs, the bandgap of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) was narrowed to 1.78 eV with the optical absorption ranges extended correspondingly. In detail, Mott-Schottky analysis was presented in Fig. 4c to obtain the flat-band potentials (Efb) of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe), which was calculated to be −0.58 eV. The positive slope linear regions of Mott-Schottky plots for N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) with different frequencies indicated the n-type nature of the sample [38]. Hence, the CB of N-CQDs/MIL-53(Fe) was calculated to be −0.54 eV vs. NHE (-0.78 eV vs. SCE) with the typical difference between the conduction band (CB) and the Efb of an n-type semiconductor being 0–0.2 eV. The valence band (VB) maximum of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) was + 1.16 eV which was determined through the empirical formula EVB = ECB + Eg. The PL spectra of MIL-53(Fe) and N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) under the excitation wavelength of 365 nm were shown in Fig. 7a. The pure MIL-53(Fe) presented a strong emission peak at around 540 nm, which was consistent with the above UV–vis results. After being decorated by N-CQDs, the PL emission intensity of the composite photocatalyst was remarkably quenched, showing that the embedding N-CQDs into MIL-53(Fe) could favor the charge separation and transfer. With the increase of the N-CQDs, PL quenching could be observed indicating an enhanced charge separation efficiency through a nonradiative quenching way [39]. This result indicated that the combination of MIL-53(Fe) and N-CQDs could effectively enhance charge separation. The photoinduced charge separation efficiency of the pure MIL-53(Fe) and N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) was further compared through electrochemical measurements. Fig. 4e displayed the transient photocurrent responses for the photocatalysts of MIL-53(Fe) and N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) over several cycles under visible-light illumination. The average photocurrent density of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) (0.15 μA cm−2) was obviously higher than that of MIL-53(Fe) (0.075 μA cm−2) under the same experimental condition. This result indicated that charge transfer and separation could be accelerated through in situ decorating N-CQDs on MIL-53(Fe) inducing a higher photocatalytic degradation rate. Fig. 4f showed the EIS Nyquist plots of pure MIL-53(Fe) and N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) [40]. Constantly, the electrical resistance was negatively correlated to the arc radius in the EIS Nyquist curves. A smaller arc radius often indicates the higher charge transfer efficiency. Obviously shown in Fig. 4f, the arc radius of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) was smaller than that of MIL-53(Fe) which indicated that the N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) had a lower resistance and better electron transfer capability.

Both concentrations of aqueous total As and As (III) were monitored to investigate the removal efficiency through photocatalytic oxidation and simultaneous adsorption. As shown in Fig. 5 a,b, the total As and As(III) concentration exhibited no significant changes in the un-absence of photocatalysis. After 120 min adsorption in dark, the concentrations of total As and As (III) in the solution were decreased from 20 to 15.64 mg L−1 and 15.56 mg L−1 over the N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) indicating the limited separately adsorption removal efficiency. Evidently shown in Fig. 5b, over 99.9 % of As (III) was removed under visible light irradiation on N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) and the concentration of total As also decreased rapidly over the catalyst with time. However, the removal efficiencies for total As and As (III) on MIL-53(Fe) were about 77.8 % and 78.2 %. The concentration of total As also decreased rapidly over the catalyst with time. The reaction rate constants (k) for the removal efficiency of total As and As(III) within the first 60 min was obtained by the equation of ln(C/C0) = -kt and the results were displayed in Fig. S4-S5 [41]. It was obvious that the N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) exhibited the highest reacting rate (k = 0.03243 min−1) which was also better than most of the reported works (shown in Table S1). As a comparison, the pure MIL-53(Fe) had a much lower reacting rate and As(III) removal efficiency than that of the N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe).

The impact of the pH value on As(III) removal was explored as the As(III) species exist in different forms in different water environments and the pH value was set at 3.2, 6.9, and 9.8 respectively [40,42]. The reaction system was kept in dark for 120 min to achieve an adsorption balance. As displayed in Fig. 5c and d, the residual concentration of total As in the solution (after 120 min reaction in in dark) was increased from 13.42 to 17.24 mg/L with the solution pH from 3.2 to 9.8. The residual concentration of As(III) in same condition was increased from 13.42 to 17.24 mg/L. This result also indicated that the N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) had a limited adsorption capacity lower than 66 mg/g. The higher adsorption capacity of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) for total As and As(III) at lower pH could be attributed to the variation of arsenic species and surface charge in solution with different pH value. In the pH range from 3.0 to 9.2, the As(III) dominantly exists in the form of H3AsO3. The adsorbed As(III) might be originated from a surface complexation mechanism associated with the surface OH− on the surface of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) [32]. With the pH increased to 9.8, As(III) was negatively charged and the electrostatic interactions prevented the adsorption of As(III) with the stronger negative charge on the surface of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe). After exposed to visible light irradiation, both the removal efficiency of As(III) and total As reached to almost 100 % over a short time with solution pH at 6.9. Distinctly, As(III) residual concentration of As(III) and total As were obviously declined with solution pH at 9.8. When solution pH was set at 3.2, the residual concentration of As(III) exhibits apparent declination while the total As residual concentration was higher. This phenomenon demonstrated that the As(III) was oxidated to As(V) under visible light irradiation with the presence of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe). Simultaneously, it also proved that a high pH value would detract the As(III) oxidation process which was also confirmed by a previous study [43]. The surface zeta potentials result of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) indicated that there were stronger electrostatic interactions for As(V) at lower pH which could cause higher total As remove efficiency. However, the lower total As residual concentration with solution pH at 6.9 certificated that the dominant As remove mechanism could be ascribed to the formation of chemical band with As at the surface of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) after releasing the proton [43]. Thereby, the association between As(V) and N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) could be enhanced by an increased pH value with higher As remove efficiency. Based on the results above, the optimal pH value was set at 6.9.

Considering the chemical complexity of natural groundwater, we also investigated the effect of coexisting cations (Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+) and anions (Cl−, CO3 2−, and SO4 2−) on As(III) removal efficiency under the photocatalytic oxidation and adsorption process. As depicted in Fig. 5c, the concentration of Na+, Ca+ and Mg2+ exhibited no significant effect on As(III) removal efficiency. Differently, the CO3 2−, and SO4 2− had an explicit inhabitation for As(III) adsorption under dark (shown in Fig. 5d) while Cl− displays a slightly discourage effect. This result could be ascribed to the occupied adsorption site of the catalyst by CO3 2−, and SO4 2− which were also proved to be the category of oxyanions. It was a worth notice that after illuminating under visible light for 120 min, their negative effect on total As removal efficiency was intensified. This phenomenon could be attributed to the competitive electrostatic interactions between the CO3 2− and SO4 2− with anions of As(V) which further prove the photocatalytic oxidate of As(III) to As(V) [44]. Previous works proved the slightly negative effect of CO3 2−, and SO4 2− on As(III) adsorption capacity was caused by the formatted unstable out-sphere complexation. The intensified negative effects on As(III) removal efficiency different from the reported work could be attributed to the essential electronegativity of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) to associate the removal of As(V).

The photocatalytic oxidate reactive stability and durability of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) were evaluated through conducting the cycle experiments of photo-oxidation and adsorption for As(III). As displayed in Fig S6, no significant attenuation of As(III) and total As remove efficiency was observed in the first three cycles indicating the pretty stability of the N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe). The As(III) and total As remove efficiency decreased to 85.4 % and 77.9 % at the fourth cycle. This result indicated that the N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) had a saturated adsorption capacity over 300 mg/g which was highly competitive to other photocatalysts (shown in Table S1). To further demonstrate the stability of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe), the leaching concentration of Fe was also tested and the results were displayed in Fig S7. Both the initial leaching concentrations of Fe for MIL-53(Fe) and N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) were lower than 200 ppb. After the reaction of adsorption and photocatalytic removal of As, the leaching concentration of Fe for both MIL-53(Fe) and N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) did not display significant increase indicating the favorable water stability. It also could be concluded that the photo-oxidation activity for As(III) of the N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) would be slightly inhibited after saturated adsorbed with As(V). By the way, the saturated adsorption capacity demonstrated that the adsorption of As(V) on N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) was tightly connected through forming a chemical band and associating with electrostatic interaction due to the rarely As loss over the repeated washing and drying process [45].

To better illustrate the mechanism of As(III) removal behavior, the pristine and reacted sample of N-CQDs/MIL-53(Fe) were investigated. Firstly, the XPS spectrums were recorded to analyze the chemical state of the sample before and after the reaction. As illustrated in Fig. 6 a, after adsorption of As (III) under dark, the peaks of C-O and CO became weaker illustrating the alter of binding energies. A similar weakening trend could also be observed in the high resolution of C 1 s spectrum after the photocatalytic oxidate of As(III). Those results indicated that the CO and CO might take part in the As(III) adsorption and photocatalytic oxidation process. From the high resolution of Fe 2p spectrum (shown in Fig. 6b), it was noticed that the binding energies of Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 were slightly shifted to higher energy after photocatalytic oxidation process of As(III) which indicated the happening of the chemical combination over the N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe). It was also noticed from the O 1 s spectrum that the newly emerging peaks of As(III)-O and As(V)-O after adsorption of and photocatalytic oxidation process of As(III). The intensity of CO peak was obviously weaker [46]. Moreover, As(III) peak appeared at 44.8 eV after adsorption of As(III) under dark while the As(V) peak emerged at and 45.7 eV after photocatalytic oxidation of As(III) under visible light (shown in Fig S9) [47]. This result confirmed that As(III) was adsorbed on the surface of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) and oxidized to As(V) completely after the photocatalytic reaction process.

Further, the dominant active species for the photocatalytic oxidation of As(III) over the N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) under visible light was also investigated through conducting the trapping experiments. During the photocatalytic oxidation process, Benzoquinone (BQ), t-tert-Butanol (TBA), and sodium oxalate (SO) were added as scavengers for superoxide radical (•O2 −), hydroxyl radical (•OH) and hole (h+), respectively. As shown in Fig. 7 b, with the addition of BQ, the both the concentrations of total As and As(III) were obviously decreased to 56.3 % and 53.5 % indicating that the •O2 − played the most important role in the photocatalytic reactions over N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe). When adding the SO as a scavenger, a slight decrease was caused for both the total As and As(III) removal efficiency. In the presence of TBA, the final remove efficiency of As(III) and total As after the photocatalytic process exhibited only a slight overshadowed than that without the presence of quencher [48]. This phenomenon indicated that the h+ also made a contribution to the photocatalytic oxidation of As(III). Therefore, it was inferred from the trapping experiments that the •O2 −, •OH and h+ were the important reactive species in the photocatalytic oxidation of As(III) over the N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe). Furthermore, the DMPO spin-trapping ESR technique was conducted to qualitatively monitor the existence of reactive species. As shown in Fig. 7c and d, the strong peak intensity of DMPO-•OH was observed after 5 min light irradiation indicating the existence of •OH. Simultaneously, the DMPO-•O2 − also presented obviously characteristic signals illustrating the accumulated generation of •O2 − in the reaction system. Based on the above results, the responsible active species for the oxidation of As(III) were •O2 − and •OH.

To deeply elucidate the precise mechanism of the As(III) removal over the N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe), adsorption free energies of As on different active sites were calculated. The adsorption free energies in aqueous solution (ΔGs) were determined according to the equation of ΔG = G p - G r, where Gr represents the free energies of all the reactants and the G p represents the sum of the free energies of all the products [49]. The energy distribution of As adsorption at different sites over N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) was shown in Fig. 8 and the site-1, site-2, site-3, site-4 represent the adsorption positions of the inner N, edge N, inner C ring, and edge C-O, respectively. Obviously, the adsorption occurred more easily at the Site-4 with the corresponding free energy order of site-4 < site-3 < site-2 < site-1 (shown in Fig. 8). It was notable that the adsorption of As(III) on the edge structure of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) was significantly stronger than that of MIL-53(Fe) which further demonstrated the positive facilitation of the introduced N-CQDs on As(III) remove. In this way, a C-O-As might be formed after the adsorption of As(III). Combined with the XPS results in Fig. 6, the adsorption mechanism of the formed O-As bonds over the N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) was obtained. This result also certificated that in situ embedding N-CQDs on MIL-53(Fe) would enrich the reaction sites thus enhancing the As(III) photocatalytic oxidation and As(V) adsorption efficiency (see Fig. 9 ).

Based on the results above and the evidence of the directly generated free radicals under visible light irradiation, a possible As(III) photocatalytic oxidation and As(V) adsorption pathway is proposed [50-52]. Firstly, after absorbing a photon, photo-generated holes (hvb +) in the conduction band (CB) and photo-generated electrons (ecb −) in the valence band (VB) were produced from the N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) under visible light irradiation. It has been demonstrated that the generate photo-generated hvb + and ecb − produced under visible light irradiation can migrate to the surface of the N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) which can be further reacted with species of oxygen and hydroxide and produce active free radicals as Eqs. (1)–(3) [53].
(1) N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) + hν → hvb + + ecb −
(2) hvb + + H2O→•OH + H+
(3) ecb − + O2→•O2 −

The free active radical of •O2 − was formed from the dissolved oxygen after primary accepted the ecb − while the •OH was produced from oxidizing adsorbed hydroxide species by the hvb +. According to Eqs. (4)–(6), both •OH and •O2 − can oxidize As(III) into As(V) . It has been reported that •OH generated from hvb + is more favorable in a neutral or basic condition.
(4) As(III) + hvb +→As(IV)
(5) As(III) + •OH → As(IV) + OH−
(6) As(IV) + •O2 − →As(V)

Followed by the reaction of the generated hvb + and •OH with As(III) respectively, the produced As(IV) can continuously react with •O2 − to generate finally As(V). Thereby, combining with the results from the quenching experiments, the responsible active species for the oxidation of As(III) was •OH and •O2 − and the photocatalytic oxidation mechanism of As(III) is demonstrated in Scheme 1.

In this work, N-CQDs was successfully in situ embedded into MIL-53(Fe) through a one-step hydrothermal solvent reaction. The oil vine-like N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) with enriched reaction sites exhibited excellent As(III) removal efficiency through the photocatalytic oxidation and simultaneous adsorption processes. The abilities of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) for As(III) oxidation and removal are 6 times greater than that of pure MIL-53(Fe). The significantly improved photocatalytic oxidation and adsorption abilities of N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) were oriented from the accelerated separation of photogenerated electron-hole by the in situ embedding N-CQDs. The adsorption mechanism of the formed O-As bonds over the N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) was obtained through DFT calculations and XPS analysis. The experimental analysis and DFT calculations revealed that the edge structure significantly improved the removal ability of As(III), which was mainly due to the increased charge transportation and improved utilization of the electrons and holes induced by the in situ embedded N-CQDs. Moreover, this oil vine-like N-CQDs3/MIL-53(Fe) expressed excellent removal efficiency in high concentrations of As(III) with good durability. This study also highlighted the properties of MIL-53(Fe) in the As(III) photocatalytic oxidation and the simultaneous adsorption of the generated arsenate [As(V)].

Haoyun Chen: Investigation. Xingzhong Yuan: Validation, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Longbo Jiang: Conceptualization, Resources. Hou Wang: Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Guanjun Zeng: Visualization, Writing – review & editing.
